text: Now here's a sporting riddle for you. American scientists once played it on snow at the South
Pole, John Prescott used to enjoy it when he was Deputy Prime Minister and Alice in
Wonderland used a hedgehog instead of a ball. We're talking croquet as Ian Winter discovered
in Worcestershire.
There are two things you should know about Broadwoods Croquet Club. First, their men's
team has just been crowned national champions and they've got the Murphy shield to prove
it. Second, though Megan's not impressed, most male members of the club appear to be
called John.
It's John Guy.
Hello John.
Hi Ian.
John Edwards.
Pleased to meet you John.
John Barber.
Hi John. And you must be?
Dave Parsons.
Nice to see you Dave.
Dave is the exception that proves the rule because there are 12 Johns among 60 members
all armed with mallets and loving every minute.
Croquet is this game of skill and strategy. It doesn't need strength, it doesn't need
superhuman knowledge.
It doesn't need stamina, muscles or speed. It's all about finesse.
Back in 1900, croquet made its one and only appearance at the Olympic Games in Paris.
Only the French entered a team so they won all the medals.
And only one spectator turned up to watch and he was English. There are no plans to
include croquet in the London Olympics 2012.
The success of Broadwoods Croquet Club is a real Janet and John story. Twelve years ago,
John cut the first court on the cricket field and they've both been hooked on the game ever
since.
We're all very competitive. It's amazing how competitive some people can be.
Are you competitive Janet?
Yes, I'm getting so.
It's not a gentleman or lady sport at all. It's very vicious. There are some very strong
hits that are made and you can go miles across the lawns.
A bit of needle then?
Very much a bit of needle. Very much so.
But really it's bags of fun and everyone at the club is revelling in their own way.
It's a very good game.
It's a very good game. It's a very good game. It's a very good game. It's a very good game.
And the players have all been in their new status as national champions. Only the very
worst of the English weather will curtail the croquet season.
Ian Winter, BBC Minutes Today, Broadwoods in Worcestershire.
